or **L-drill** is a test performed by [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") players. It is primarily run to evaluate the agility, quickness and fluidity of movement of players by [scouts](/wiki/Scout_(sport) "Scout (sport)"). It is most commonly seen at the [NFL Combine](/wiki/NFL_Combine "NFL Combine") in preparation for the [NFL draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft") but is also an important measurement for [collegiate](/wiki/College_football "College football") [recruiting](/wiki/Recruiting_(athletics) "Recruiting (athletics)"). While not as highly regarded a test as the [40-yard dash](/wiki/40-yard_dash "40-yard dash"), it is still an important measure used by team personnel to compare players. It is especially pertinent in the evaluation of [pass rushers](/wiki/Pass_rush "Pass rush") who must be able to maintain acceleration while working around [offensive line](/wiki/Offensive_line "Offensive line") players.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The drill was invented by football scout [C. O. Brocato](/wiki/C._O._Brocato "C. O. Brocato").[[2]](#cite_note-2)
["3 Cone Drill"](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=OmvERmXLlmU). *NFL.com*. Retrieved June 10, 2020.[*[dead YouTube link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Test in American football

The **three-cone drill**, **3-cone drill** or **L-drill** is a test performed by [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") players. It is primarily run to evaluate the agility, quickness and fluidity of movement of players by [scouts](/wiki/Scout_(sport) "Scout (sport)"). It is most commonly seen at the [NFL Combine](/wiki/NFL_Combine "NFL Combine") in preparation for the [NFL draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft") but is also an important measurement for [collegiate](/wiki/College_football "College football") [recruiting](/wiki/Recruiting_(athletics) "Recruiting (athletics)"). While not as highly regarded a test as the [40-yard dash](/wiki/40-yard_dash "40-yard dash"), it is still an important measure used by team personnel to compare players. It is especially pertinent in the evaluation of [pass rushers](/wiki/Pass_rush "Pass rush") who must be able to maintain acceleration while working around [offensive line](/wiki/Offensive_line "Offensive line") players.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The drill was invented by football scout [C. O. Brocato](/wiki/C._O._Brocato "C. O. Brocato").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## The drill

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Three-cone_drill&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: The drill")]

Three cones are placed five yards apart from each other forming a [right angle](/wiki/Right_angle "Right angle"). The athlete starts with one hand down on the ground and runs to the middle cone and touches it. The player then reverses direction back to the starting cone and touches it. The athlete reverses direction again but this time runs around the outside of the middle cone on the way to the far cone running around it in figure eight fashion on his way back around the outside of the middle cornering cone. Athletes are timed for this whole procedure. This drill is primarily used to determine a player's [agility](/wiki/Agility "Agility").[[3]](#cite_note-3)
